message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The immunogenicity of the BNT162b2 COVID-19 vaccine is understudied in elderly people with comorbidities. We assessed SARS-CoV-2-S-targeted antibody and T cell responses following full vaccination in nursing home residents (NHR).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We recruited 60 NHR (44 female; median age, 87.5 years), of whom 10 had previously had COVID-19, and 18 healthy controls (15 female; median age, 48.5 years). Pre- and post-vaccination blood specimens were available for quantitation of total antibodies binding RBD and enumeration of SARS-CoV-2-S-reactive IFN-γ CD4
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    and CD8
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    T cells by flow cytometry.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The seroconversion rate in presumably SARS-CoV-2 naïve NHR (95.3%), either with or without comorbidities, was similar to controls (94.4%). A robust booster effect was documented in NHR with prior COVID-19. Plasma antibody levels were higher in convalescent NHR than in individuals across the other two groups. A large percentage of NHR had SARS-CoV-2 S-reactive IFN-γ CD8
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    and/or CD4
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    T cells at baseline, in contrast to healthy controls. Either CD8
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    and/or CD4
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    T-cell responses were documented in all control subjects after vaccination. Contrariwise, the percentage of NHR exhibiting detectable SARS-CoV-2 IFN-γ CD8
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    or CD4
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    T-cell responses (or both), irrespective of their baseline SARS-CoV-2 infection status, dropped consistently after vaccination. Overall, SARS-CoV-2 IFN-γ CD8
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    and CD4
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    T-cell responses in NHR decreased in post-vaccination specimens.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The BNT162b2 COVID-19 vaccine elicits robust SARS-CoV-2-S antibody responses in NHR. Nevertheless, the frequency and magnitude of detectable SARS-CoV-2 IFN-γ T-cell responses after vaccination was lower in NHR compared to controls.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: B and T cell immune responses elicited by the BNT162b2 (Pfizer–BioNTech) COVID-19 vaccine in nursing home residents

reference.article-title: COVID-19 vaccine BNT162b1 elicits human antibody and TH1 T cell responses
